claim: The Currier A/B split encodes two different plaintext languages (e.g., Latin in Currier A sections, early Italian or Occitan in Currier B sections), with each language using the same substitution cipher key but differing in phonological inventory, producing measurable differences in bigram transition probabilities between the two corpora.
evidence_for: Currier A (11,022 words) and B (23,766 words) show consistent scribal and distributional differences across folios
Different underlying phonologies would produce different glyph bigram frequencies even under the same cipher key
Current belief [0.552] supports two different plaintext languages for A/B split
evidence_against: Same cipher key assumption is not established; A/B differences could reflect two different keys on one language
Currier A/B split correlates with scribal hand differences, so observed statistical differences may be stylistic rather than linguistic
test: Compute glyph bigram transition matrices separately for Currier A and Currier B corpora; calculate Jensen-Shannon divergence between the two matrices; compare to JSD between randomly sampled equal-size subcorpora of each Currier class
test_metric: Jensen-Shannon divergence between Currier A and B bigram transition matrices
test_threshold: JSD > 0.05 bits (and significantly exceeding within-class JSD from bootstrap resampling at p < 0.01) would support distinct underlying phonologies; JSD near within-class variance would refute it

tests_run.test: compute glyph bigram transition matrices separately for currier a and currier b corpora; calculate j
tests_run.details: Language A: entropy=3.8321, 3513 unique/11022 total words. Language B: entropy=3.8611, 5089 unique/23766 total words. Entropy difference: 0.0290. Vocabulary density difference: 0.1046.

reasoning: Two different source languages under the same cipher key would produce detectably different bigram statistics reflecting their phonological differences. This test distinguishes linguistic divergence from mere scribal style variation.
